title: Odd-parity magnons in the Haldane-Hubbard model from topological exciton condensation
abstract: Odd-wave magnets are the counterparts to even-wave altermagnets realizing odd-parity spin splitting. Normally discussed for noncollinear systems, they have recently been shown to appear in collinear magnetic states in the presence of loop currents. Here we study collective excitations of the paramagnetic and magnetic phase of the seminal Haldane-Hubbard model. We identify the existence topological excitons in the paramagnetic phase, and their condensation as the driving mechanism into the collinear Néel state. The latter realizes an odd-wave magnet with odd-parity magnons displaying a characteristic $f$-wave splitting. We further uncover that an electron bandgap closing ensures magnon bandgap closing causing a change in odd-parity magnon topology, as well as a drastically enlarged spin splitting. Our results establish the presence of topological excitons and odd-parity magnons in the Haldane-Hubbard, with potential realizations in Floquet-driven materials and cold atomic gases.
summary: This work analyzes the Haldane-Hubbard model to show that topological excitons condense, driving the system into a novel odd-parity magnetic state. This resulting magnet exhibits characteristic f-wave magnon splitting, and the theory predicts how changes in electronic band topology govern the magnon's quantum properties.
main_problem: The study investigates the collective excitations (excitons and magnons) and the mechanism driving the transition into an odd-parity magnet within the Haldane-Hubbard model.
main_result: The condensation of topological excitons drives the system into a collinear Néel state, realizing an odd-wave magnet characterized by f-wave magnon splitting. Furthermore, electron bandgap closing dictates changes in magnon topology and enhances spin splitting.
method: Theoretical analysis using Time-Dependent Hartree–Fock (TDHF) and Random Phase Approximation (RPA) to calculate exciton and magnon spectra.
model_or_system: The Haldane-Hubbard model is studied on a honeycomb lattice, incorporating nearest-neighbor ($t_1$) and next-nearest-neighbor hopping ($t_2$) with loop currents, alongside an onsite Hubbard repulsion ($U$).
key_observables: Dynamical spin structure factor $S(q, \omega)$, exciton Berry curvature ($	ext{BC}$), magnon dispersion relation, and topological invariants (Chern numbers).
important_parameters: Half-filled case; intermediate interaction regime ($U/t_1 = 4$); lattice geometry (honeycomb).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mean-field descriptions (TDHF, RPA) to describe the excitonic condensation transition.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate $S(q, \omega)$ in paramagnetic and AFM phases, show exciton Berry curvature distribution, and map out electron band structures near phase boundaries.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops the theoretical framework by first establishing the model Hamiltonian, then using TDHF/RPA to calculate excitonic properties (including Chern numbers), demonstrating how exciton condensation drives the magnetic transition into the odd-parity magnon state, and finally analyzing the consequences of bandgap closing.
why_interesting: The explicit connection drawn between topological exciton condensation in an itinerant model and the emergence of topologically non-trivial magnons is highly relevant to understanding emergent quantum phenomena in correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. (a),(b) Dynamical spin structure factor S (q, ω) = S +−(q, ω) + S −+(q, ω) of the HH model, overlaid with the TDHF spectra in the topological paramagnetic phase A. Colored dashed lines and shaded regions represent isolated collective modes and particle-hole continua, respectively. (c),(d) Same as (a),(b), but for the topological odd-parity-wave AFM phase B, together with the spin-resolved TDHF spectra. The inset in (d) shows the isoenergy contour of the TDHF spectrum at ω/t1 = 1, corresponding to the white dashed lines in (c) and (d). For all panels, U/t1 = 4 and ϕ = π/2. The inset in (g) shows the isoenergy contour of the electron bands at ε/t1 = 1.5, corresponding to the gray dashed line.
